title: Isoproterenol: nonselective beta agonist
vignette: A 66-year-old man is evaluated for recurrent syncope. He has had 3 episodes of dizziness and palpitations followed by brief loss of consciousness over the last 6 months. He has no chest pain or dyspnea. The patient has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia. There is no family history of sudden death. Initial evaluation shows normal ECG and echocardiogram. A cardiac electrophysiologic study is performed. During the study, intravenous infusion of a medication is administered and produces an increase in cardiac contractility and a decrease in vascular resistance, as shown in the graphs. Which of the following medications was most likely administered to this patient?

clues.note: The clinical setup (EP study for syncope) is just a vehicle to administer a drug and study its effects. The diagnosis matters less than reading the hemodynamic response.
Increased contractility (positive inotropy) reflects beta-1 adrenergic stimulation on the heart. This is one of the two responses to match to a drug.

Decreased systemic vascular resistance reflects beta-2 mediated vasodilation of vascular smooth muscle. Increased contractility (beta-1) PLUS decreased resistance (beta-2), with no alpha effect, points to a NONSELECTIVE BETA agonist — isoproterenol.

whatMatters: Ignore the syncope wrapper — the answer comes from the two graphed effects: increased contractility (beta-1) and decreased vascular resistance (beta-2). A drug that does both with negligible alpha activity is isoproterenol, a nonselective beta agonist (beta-1 + beta-2). Epinephrine/norepinephrine/phenylephrine would raise resistance via alpha; cholinergics/clonidine/esmolol don't fit.
strategy: For adrenergic-drug graphs, read the receptor signature: beta-1 = increased HR/contractility; beta-2 = vasodilation (decreased SVR/diastolic BP); alpha-1 = vasoconstriction (increased SVR). Increased contractility + decreased SVR with no rise in resistance = pure beta (beta-1 + beta-2) = isoproterenol.

eliminations.label: acetylcholine — decreases HR/contractility
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: acetylcholine acts on cardiac M2 (slows HR/conduction) and vascular M3 (endothelial NO → vasodilation). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it DECREASES heart rate/contractility rather than increasing contractility; the inotropic effect is wrong. ✅ Correct if: the graphs showed decreased HR/contractility with vasodilation (muscarinic).

eliminations.label: adenosine — transient AV block, not positive inotropy
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: adenosine briefly blocks AV nodal conduction (used to terminate SVT) and causes flushing/vasodilation. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it doesn't increase contractility; its hallmark is transient bradycardia/AV block. ✅ Correct if: the goal were terminating a reentrant SVT with transient asystole.

eliminations.label: clonidine — central alpha-2 agonist, lowers sympathetic output
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: clonidine stimulates central alpha-2 receptors to REDUCE sympathetic outflow (lowers HR and BP). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it would decrease contractility and sympathetic tone, not increase contractility. ✅ Correct if: the drug lowered BP via reduced central sympathetic outflow.

eliminations.label: esmolol — beta-1 BLOCKER, opposite effect
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: esmolol is a short-acting beta-1 antagonist. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: blocking beta-1 DECREASES contractility — the exact opposite of the graphed increase. ✅ Correct if: the response were decreased HR/contractility (beta blockade).

eliminations.label: norepinephrine — alpha-driven INCREASE in resistance
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: norepinephrine is a potent alpha-1 (and beta-1) agonist with little beta-2 effect. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it RAISES vascular resistance via alpha-1 (vasoconstriction), opposite to the graphed DECREASE in resistance. ✅ Correct if: the graph showed increased SVR/BP with reflex bradycardia (alpha-1 predominant).

eliminations.label: phenylephrine — pure alpha-1, raises resistance
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: phenylephrine is a selective alpha-1 agonist (vasoconstrictor, nasal decongestant, pressor). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it INCREASES vascular resistance and causes reflex bradycardia — opposite of decreased resistance and increased contractility. ✅ Correct if: the graph showed increased SVR/BP with reflex bradycardia (pure alpha-1).

reasoning: Graphs: increased contractility (beta-1) + decreased vascular resistance (beta-2)
No rise in resistance means negligible alpha-1 activity
A nonselective beta-1 + beta-2 agonist fits both
mechanism: Isoproterenol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic agonist (beta-1 and beta-2) with negligible alpha activity. Beta-1 stimulation increases heart rate and contractility (positive inotropy/chronotropy); beta-2 stimulation relaxes vascular smooth muscle, lowering systemic vascular resistance and diastolic blood pressure. The combination — increased contractility with decreased resistance and no vasoconstriction — is the signature that distinguishes it from alpha-active agents (norepinephrine, phenylephrine, epinephrine).

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Pharm > adrenergic agonists table: alpha-1 (vasoconstriction), alpha-2 (decrease sympathetic outflow), beta-1 (HR/contractility), beta-2 (vasodilation/bronchodilation); examples (epi, NE, phenylephrine, isoproterenol, dobutamine, clonidine).

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Read the two graphed effects as receptor fingerprints (contractility = beta-1; vasodilation = beta-2), then find the drug hitting both with no alpha. Match physiology to receptors before scanning names.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on inotropy/chronotropy, vascular resistance, beta-1 vs beta-2 vs alpha-1, nonselective beta agonist? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Norepinephrine and phenylephrine tempt as 'adrenergic,' but their alpha-1 RAISES resistance. The graphs show DECREASED resistance — only a pure beta (beta-1+beta-2) agonist (isoproterenol) fits.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: The reference is the adrenergic-drug effects table; the graphs plot contractility (up) and vascular resistance (down) over time. Map each curve to its receptor (beta-1 up, beta-2 down) to ID isoproterenol.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Tell me what happened (timing, second-guessing, careless click) and I'll suggest a fix for future questions.

firstAid.detail: Pharmacology > sympathomimetics: isoproterenol (nonselective beta), dobutamine (beta-1), receptor effects on contractility and vascular resistance.
firstAid.location: Pharmacology > sympathomimetics: isoproterenol (nonselective beta), dobutamine (beta-1), receptor effects on contractility and vascular resistance.

firstAid.detail: Pharmacology > adrenergic agonist/antagonist table (epinephrine, NE, phenylephrine, clonidine, esmolol) and acetylcholine/adenosine cardiac effects.
firstAid.location: Pharmacology > adrenergic agonist/antagonist table (epinephrine, NE, phenylephrine, clonidine, esmolol) and acetylcholine/adenosine cardiac effects.

firstAid.detail: Physiology > alpha-1/alpha-2/beta-1/beta-2 receptor locations and second messengers — to read drug-effect graphs.
firstAid.location: Physiology > alpha-1/alpha-2/beta-1/beta-2 receptor locations and second messengers — to read drug-effect graphs.

memoryHook: ISO = 'I Stimulate Only beta' (beta-1 ↑contractility + beta-2 ↓resistance), no alpha — so resistance FALLS instead of rising.
